summary: Gruber catalogs his interface gripes with Apple's Safari browser, prompted by Dave Hyatt's open call for GUI suggestions. He criticizes several specific design decisions: a non-functional 'Show' label in the View menu that violates Apple's own Human Interface Guidelines, the combined Stop/Reload button that can trigger the opposite action, the lack of external View Source support, the inline Bookmarks window that creates confusion, hardcoded blue button graphics that ignore the user's Graphite appearance setting, and the brushed metal window skin. Throughout, he appeals to Apple's own HIG documentation as the standard Safari should meet.
key_points: The 'Show' label in Safari's View menu violates the HIG principle that menu items must be commands, and is unnecessary since 'View' already serves as the implicit verb
The combined Stop/Reload button is a clever space-saver that creates a dangerous race condition where clicking a fraction too late triggers the exact opposite action
Safari's inline Bookmarks display causes confusion because the window title and address bar still show the hidden page, and the bookmarks window doesn't appear in the Windows list
Safari's hardcoded blue Address Bar button graphics ignore the user's system-wide Graphite appearance preference, violating Apple's own HIG guidance on custom controls
Gruber repeatedly cites Apple's Human Interface Guidelines to hold Safari accountable to Apple's own stated standards
money_quotes: Less than one day later, there are already over 600 comments, most of which are asking for tabbed browsing. Jesus, what a great idea! Why didn't anyone suggest that before?
I don't know where this little turd came from, but it should be removed.
Menu items are commands. No ifs ands or buts. There should never be a menu item that is anything other than a menu item.
Guess what happens if you try to hit Stop, but you're a fraction of a second too late? You wanted to stop, but the exact opposite happens.
I've tried to like this, I've really tried, but I don't.
You know it's open, so you click-and-hold on the Safari icon in your Dock. The contextual menu lists all your open windows, but none of them are called 'Bookmarks'.
key_insight: Apple's own Safari browser violates Apple's own Human Interface Guidelines in multiple ways, and holding software to its platform's design standards is how you get better software.
